title: Communication in Organizations
simple_html:
Communication is how information, meaning, and emotion move between people in an organization. It is the connective tissue of management: nothing gets coordinated, decided, or led without it. Studies routinely find managers spend the majority of their day communicating, so doing it well is a core leadership skill.

The basic model

Communication is a loop. A sender has an idea, encodes it into words or signals, sends it through a channel (email, meeting, chat), and a receiver decodes it. The receiver then gives feedback so the sender knows whether the message landed. Anything that distorts the message along the way is called noise — literal noise, jargon, distractions, or emotional bias.

Direction and channels

Messages flow downward (boss to team), upward (team to boss), and horizontally (peer to peer). Each channel has different richness — its ability to carry tone, nuance, and instant feedback. A face-to-face talk is rich; a mass email is lean. A good rule: use rich channels for ambiguous, emotional, or important messages, and lean channels for simple, routine ones.

Why messages fail

Common barriers include filtering (people tell the boss what they want to hear), information overload, selective perception (we hear what fits our expectations), and poor active listening.

Key takeaways

  • Communication is a two-way loop — feedback proves the message was understood, not just sent.
  • Match the channel to the message: rich channels for complex or sensitive content.
  • Noise and filtering distort meaning; reduce them with clarity and trust.
  • Active listening (attending, paraphrasing, withholding judgment) is half the skill.
  • Nonverbal cues often carry more weight than the words themselves.
intermediate_html:
Communication in organizations is the process of transferring and understanding meaning. Because coordination, motivation, decision-making, and control all run on it, communication competence is one of the strongest predictors of managerial effectiveness. This lesson lays out the core process, channel theory, communication networks, and the barriers that cause messages to fail.

The communication process model

The classic Shannon-Weaver-derived model treats communication as a sequence: sender → encoding → message → channel → decoding → receiver → feedback, with noise potentially distorting any stage. Encoding is converting thought into transmittable symbols; decoding is the receiver's interpretation. The model's key insight is that meaning is reconstructed by the receiver, not delivered intact — so the sender's intent and the receiver's interpretation can diverge sharply.

Channel richness

Media richness theory ranks channels by their capacity to convey information: the ability to handle multiple cues simultaneously, give rapid feedback, and feel personal. From richest to leanest: face-to-face > video > phone > instant message > email > formal report/memo. The managerial guideline is a fit rule: non-routine, ambiguous, emotionally charged messages (firing someone, resolving conflict, announcing change) demand rich channels; routine, clear messages (a meeting time, a policy reminder) are efficiently handled by lean channels. Mismatches cause trouble — layoffs by email feel callous; scheduling a meeting by calling everyone wastes a rich channel.

Direction of flow and networks

Formal communication flows downward (goals, instructions, feedback), upward (status, ideas, grievances — often suppressed by fear), and laterally (peer coordination). Beyond formal channels sits the grapevine, the informal network that is fast, largely accurate, and impossible to eliminate — smart managers monitor it. Small-group network research distinguishes the chain (rigid hierarchy), wheel (a central hub), and all-channel (everyone talks to everyone) patterns. The all-channel network maximizes member satisfaction and speed for complex tasks; the wheel is fast and accurate for simple tasks with a strong leader.

Barriers and how to overcome them

  • Filtering — senders manipulate information so it looks favorable (worse with tall hierarchies). Counter with psychological safety and multiple feedback routes.
  • Selective perception — receivers project their own expectations onto messages.
  • Information overload — beyond capacity, people drop, ignore, or err. Counter by prioritizing and curating.
  • Emotions and language — jargon and strong feelings distort encoding and decoding.
  • Silence — the absence of communication is itself meaningful data that is easy to miss.

Active listening and feedback

Receiving is an active skill: attend (focus, eye contact), paraphrase to confirm understanding, ask clarifying questions, and withhold premature judgment. Effective feedback is specific, timely, focused on behavior (not personality), and ideally descriptive rather than evaluative.

Worked mini-case

A regional manager must announce a restructuring that eliminates two roles. She emails the full team a cheerful summary. Result: rumors explode on the grapevine, the two affected employees feel blindsided, and trust drops. Diagnosis: a highly ambiguous, emotional message was pushed through a lean channel with no feedback loop, maximizing noise and filtering. Better approach: face-to-face individual conversations with the affected employees first (richest channel, immediate feedback), then a team meeting to address questions, with email used only afterward to document agreed facts. The fix is not more communication but the right channel matched to the message.

How to apply it

Before any important message, ask: What is my real goal? How ambiguous or emotional is this? Which channel fits? How will I get feedback that it was understood? Have I removed jargon and reduced noise? This four-question discipline converts communication from an afterthought into a deliberate managerial tool.
full_html:
Communication — the transfer and shared understanding of meaning — is the substrate on which every other organizational behavior process runs. Mintzberg's (1973) observational studies of managerial work found that managers spend up to 80% of their time communicating, and decades of research link communication competence to leadership emergence, team performance, and employee engagement. This lesson develops the full architecture of organizational communication: the process model and its limits, media richness and channel selection, formal and informal networks, persistent barriers, listening and feedback, nonverbal and cross-cultural dimensions, and the contemporary shift to digital and virtual communication.

The process model and its theoretical roots

The dominant transmission model derives from Shannon and Weaver's (1949) mathematical theory of communication: an information source encodes a message, transmits it over a channel subject to noise, and a receiver decodes it. Organizational behavior adapts this into the sender → encoding → message → channel → decoding → receiver → feedback loop. The model's enduring value is its diagnostic power — failures can be localized to encoding (poor word choice), channel (wrong medium), decoding (misinterpretation), or noise (distraction, bias). Its enduring limitation, critiqued by interpretivists, is that it implies meaning is a fixed package transmitted intact. The constructivist counter-view holds that meaning is co-constructed: the receiver actively builds an interpretation from prior schemas, so identical messages yield different meanings across receivers. Weick's (1979) concept of sensemaking pushes this further — organizations don't merely transmit pre-existing meaning, they enact reality through ongoing communication.

Media richness and channel selection

Daft and Lengel's (1986) media richness theory is the central applied framework. A channel's richness is its capacity to (1) carry multiple cues (tone, body language), (2) provide immediate feedback, (3) use natural language, and (4) convey personal focus. The richness hierarchy runs face-to-face > video > telephone > synchronous chat > email > impersonal written documents. The core proposition is a fit hypothesis: equivocal, ambiguous tasks require rich media to reduce multiple competing interpretations, whereas merely uncertain tasks (needing more facts) are served by lean media. Performance suffers from mismatch in either direction — using a lean channel for an equivocal task (announcing layoffs by email) leaves interpretation chaotic, while overusing rich channels for routine matters wastes time. Later work on channel expansion theory (Carlson & Zmud, 1999) qualified the static hierarchy: experienced users of a medium (e.g., a long-time email correspondent with a known colleague) can extract richness a novice cannot, so richness is partly learned, not intrinsic.

Direction, networks, and the grapevine

Formal flows are downward (directives, feedback, indoctrination), upward (status reporting, suggestions, grievances), and horizontal/lateral (peer coordination that bypasses slow vertical routing). Upward communication is chronically distorted by the MUM effect — the reluctance to transmit unfavorable news upward — which feeds organizational blind spots. The informal grapevine is fast, roughly 75% accurate on facts, and arises whenever official information is scarce or anxiety is high (Davis, 1953); it cannot be abolished, only managed by transparency. Classic small-group network experiments (Leavitt, 1951; Bavelas, 1950) distinguish the chain, Y, wheel, circle, and all-channel structures. The centralized wheel solves simple problems fastest and most accurately but yields low member satisfaction and poor performance on complex problems; the decentralized all-channel is slower to organize but superior for complex, novel tasks and raises satisfaction. The managerial lesson is structural contingency: centralize communication for simple/urgent tasks, decentralize for complex/creative ones.

Barriers to effective communication

Robbins and Judge catalog the recurring barriers: filtering (purposeful manipulation of information, worsening with hierarchy depth); selective perception (receivers see what their needs and expectations dictate); information overload (beyond cognitive capacity, people forget, ignore, or err — an acute issue in the always-on digital workplace); emotions (extreme affect substitutes for rational interpretation); language (jargon, age, and subculture create divergent word-meanings); and silence, whose informational content is routinely overlooked. Communication apprehension — situational anxiety about oral or written communication — leads affected individuals to avoid rich channels, distorting their information flow.

Listening, feedback, and nonverbal communication

Reception is not passive. Active listening entails intensity, empathy, acceptance, and a willingness to take responsibility for completeness — operationally: attending, paraphrasing, asking clarifying questions, and deferring evaluation. Effective feedback is specific, behavior-focused (not person-focused), well-timed, and — per the descriptive/evaluative distinction — framed around observed actions rather than character judgments. Nonverbal communication (kinesics, proxemics, paralanguage) often dominates: Mehrabian's (1971) frequently-cited (and frequently-misapplied) finding that meaning in emotionally inconsistent messages is conveyed 7% by words, 38% by tone, 55% by body language underscores that how something is said can override what is said. Note the common misconception: Mehrabian's ratios apply specifically to feelings/attitudes where verbal and nonverbal cues conflict, not to all communication.

Cross-cultural and digital dimensions

Hall's (1976) distinction between high-context cultures (much meaning resides in shared context, relationships, and nonverbal cues — e.g., Japan, Arab states) and low-context cultures (meaning is explicit in the words — e.g., Germany, the US) explains many cross-cultural misfires; a direct, explicit American memo can read as blunt or distrustful in a high-context setting. The digital shift — email, enterprise social platforms, and especially virtual teams (see Lesson 5) — strips nonverbal cues, raising the risk of misattributed tone, and concentrates communication in leaner channels, which media richness theory predicts will impair equivocal tasks unless deliberately countered with periodic rich-channel contact.

Worked example

A multinational's HQ (low-context, US) emails its Tokyo subsidiary (high-context, Japan) a terse directive to "cut costs 10% immediately, report numbers Friday." Tokyo perceives the lean channel and blunt phrasing as disrespectful and ambiguous about how, replies with vague optimism (MUM effect + face concerns), and HQ misreads the vagueness as compliance. Diagnosis: a high-equivocality, cross-cultural message sent on a lean, low-context channel maximized noise, filtering, and decoding error. Remedy: a video meeting (rich channel) opened with relationship-building, explicit invitation of upward feedback, and culturally calibrated framing — followed by written documentation only to confirm agreed specifics.

Common mistakes

  • Equating sending with communicating — ignoring the feedback loop that proves understanding.
  • Defaulting to email/lean channels for emotional or ambiguous messages because they are convenient.
  • Treating the grapevine as noise to suppress rather than a signal to read and a channel to inform.
  • Over-applying Mehrabian's 7-38-55 ratio to all messages.
  • Assuming a message understood in one's own culture transfers cleanly across high/low-context divides.

Cross-links

Communication underpins Groups & Teams (Lesson 5, especially virtual-team coordination), is the delivery mechanism for every Leadership behavior and vision (Lesson 7), is the medium of Power, Politics & Conflict — influence tactics and negotiation are applied communication (Lesson 8) — and is the primary lever for transmitting Organizational Culture and managing resistance during Change (Lesson 9). It also connects upstream to Perception (Lesson 2), since decoding is fundamentally a perceptual act.
